coredata.dc:title: Preparation of functionalized double ratio fluorescent imprinted sensors for visual determination and recognition of dopamine in human serum
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yeqing
Wang, Jixiang
Lu, Yang
Dai, Xiaohui
Yan, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorescent sensors have shown great prospect in chemical monitoring and recognition due to its high intuitiveness, accurateness, and visualization. In this work, the ratiometric fluorescent sensors, which includes a blue fluorescent Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as internal standard material, and a red fluorescent boric acid-modified CdTe QDs as response signal. Then we choose dopamine (DA) as template, 3-phenylboronic acid (APBA) for functional monomers, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) for cross-linker to synthesize double ratio molecularly imprinted polymers (DR-MIPs) that can identify dopamine selectively and sensitively. The DR-MIPs has better capability of selective recognition, obvious anti-ion interference, rapid detection and good visualization. Furthermore, the unique DR-MIPs was proved as efficient visual sensors for determination of DA in human serum rapidly and efficiently. The DR-MIPs still displayed well accuracy, and the potential prospects of this smart sensor is clearly demonstrated in the context of modern clinical medicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine
Quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescent
Molecularly imprinted
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study of pH and detection time
Cis-Diol substance detection of DR-MIPs detection system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation of the synthetic process of DR-MIPs.
(A) TEM image of DR-MIPs, (B) FI-IR spectra of DR-MIPs.

Fluorescence spectra of (a) red-emission CdTe QDs, (b) DR-MIPs and (c) blue-emission CQDs. The inset shows the photographs of (a) red-emission CdTe QDs, (b) DR-MIPs and (c) blue emission CQDs under daylight and a 365 nm UV lamp respectively.
(a)Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of DR-MIPs (black line) and DR-NIPs (red line). (b)Fluorescence intensity of DR-MIPs in the presence of 200 μM DA at different incubation time.
Fluorescence spectra and corresponding photographs of DR-MIPs (a), DR-NIPs (b) and CdTe QDs-APBA@SiO2 (c) upon the exposure to different concentrations of DA.
Fluorescence intensity ratio (I651/I448) of DR-MIPs at 3.5 μM Al3+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, K+, Mn2+, Ba2+ and DA, the inset was the actual picture of these substances.
Fluorescence spectra of DR-MIPs of different targets at the same concentrations (range from 0 to 5 μM).
Results of DA determination in human serum samples.
DA determination of different works compared with this work.

head.ce:title: Preparation of functionalized double ratio fluorescent imprinted sensors for visual determination and recognition of dopamine in human serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Jilin normal University, Siping, 136000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yangzhong Tiande Electrical Equipment Co.LTD, Zhengjiang 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric fluorescent sensors have shown great prospect in chemical monitoring and recognition due to its high intuitiveness, accurateness, and visualization. In this work, the ratiometric fluorescent sensors, which includes a blue fluorescent Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as internal standard material, and a red fluorescent boric acid-modified CdTe QDs as response signal. Then we choose dopamine (DA) as template, 3-phenylboronic acid (APBA) for functional monomers, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) for cross-linker to synthesize double ratio molecularly imprinted polymers (DR-MIPs) that can identify dopamine selectively and sensitively. The DR-MIPs has better capability of selective recognition, obvious anti-ion interference, rapid detection and good visualization. Furthermore, the unique DR-MIPs was proved as efficient visual sensors for determination of DA in human serum rapidly and efficiently. The DR-MIPs still displayed well accuracy, and the potential prospects of this smart sensor is clearly demonstrated in the context of modern clinical medicine.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a neurotransmitter of catecholamine, which dramatically influences a variety of life processes including central nervous system, hormones and the cardiovascular system. [1,2] Excessive secretion of DA may lead to metabolic disorders and even life-threatening health. However, the loss of DA may lead to uncontrolled muscle mass, impaired attention, and even Parkinson's syndrome. [3] Therefore, finding a way to survey and evaluate determination of DA in the organism is very important for the prediction of disease. Up to now, through the efforts of many researchers, there are many ways to detect DA, such as electrochemical [4–6], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], chemiluminescence [8,9], UV detection [1,10,11] and enzyme catalysis [12], etc. These methods can accurately detect the content of DA in the general sample. However, with the rapid development of science and technology and modern standard of living, higher requirements for the rapidity, accuracy and conciseness of dopamine detection are in urgent need in clinical medicine. Nevertheless, these methodology inevitably have some disadvantages, such as expensive instruments cost, long sample pretreatment and secondary pollutants of solvent. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple, rapid, sensitive and efficient method for the qualitative and quantitative detection of DA in modern clinical medicine.

As everyone knows, the fluorescence analytical method have been the focus of analytical chemistry, biomedicine and materials research, which have the properties of high efficiency, simplicity and sensitivity [13]. Quantum dots (QDs) have many advantages, such as broad excitation spectrum, narrow and symmetrical emission spectrum, controllable emission wavelength, higher quantum yield, better optical stability and so on [14,15]. It is an ideal fluorescent nanomaterial. Based on these unique optical properties of QDs, it has been widely used in many fields, such as analysis detection and biological imaging. [16–18] Due to the unique neighboring hydroxyl structure of DA, APBA is chosen to be the functional monomer of DR-MIPs. The amidogen of APBA can connect with carboxyl of QDs' surface, and the boric acid radical of APBA can combine with DA by covalent bonding, then the tetraethyl orthosilicate plays a role of cross-linker agent to acquire DR-MIPs after removing DA, to realize the selective recognition of DA. In order to establish a better and more intuitive fluorescent recognition system, ratiometric fluorescent sensors were developed for the visual detection of DA based on double ratio molecularly imprinted polymers. Compared with the common fluorescence analysis method, the ratiometric fluorescent sensors are more accurate and visualized. [19,20] Thus, this ratiometric fluorescent sensors has a wider application prospect. So far, there have been many reports about surface functionalized QDs fluorescent probes. [21,22] Zhang and co-workers [23] prepared a CdTe QDs ratiometric fluorescent sensor with red fluorescence and green fluorescence for detection of TNT. Wang et al. [24] prepared a red and green fluorescence CdTe QDs ratiometric fluorescent sensor to detect cysteine and homocysteine. Liu and co-workers [25] synthetized 3- amino boronic acid- modified CuInS2 QDs for the detection of DA. Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) is a method that combines substrate with template molecule to obtain polymer under the action of crosslinking agent. After eluting template molecule, molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs) with specific imprinting hole can be obtained, template molecule can be specifically recognized. Applying MIT into ratiometric fluorescent sensors, the template molecule can be detected by onsite naked eyes, and detected selectively at the same time. Lu et al. [26] using AuNPs as seeds and glucose for the precursor of CQDs, designed dual-emission nanoparticles using the co-microwave seed growth method, then synthesized molecularly imprinted ratio fluorescence sensors in the presence of bisphenol A (BPA) and CTAB, finally realized the quantitative detection of BPA.

In this work, the functional monomer APBA and fluorescent group CdTe QDs were added first to modify CdTe QDs with boric acid groups, and then another fluorescent group CQDs, template molecule DA and crosslinking agent TEOS were added to polymerization, after removing DA, the special recognition sites were leaved on the surface of polymers, the DR-MIPs were obtained, when DA was added again, DR-MIPs could rebind DA selectively and quickly, DA-induced quenching of the red-light emission and subsequently changes of the hybrid fluorescence color, eventually to realize the visual and selective detection of DA. The optical properties of ratiometric fluorescence probe and its visual effects on DA were studied systematically in this work. This unique DR-MIPs was proved as efficient sensor for the determination of DA in human serum. This work thus offers a facile and efficient strategy for rapid, visual, and on-site detection of DA in modern clinical medicine.

Mercaptoacetic acid, CdCl2·2.5H2O, NaBH4, tellurium powder, citric acid monohydrate, polyethyleneimine (PEI), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), NH3·H2O, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), dopamine (DA) and catechol, were all purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaOH, ethyl alcohol, 3-phenylboronic acid (APBA), gallic acid were obtained from Guoyao chemical reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and solvents used were prepared from analytical grade. Distilled water was used for preparing all aqueous solutions and cleaning processes. All reagents are analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

The model of heating magnetic stirrer is RET basic (IKA, Germany). The electronic balance model is TE124S (Sartorius, Germany). The structure and composition analysis of nanomaterial was investigated by Nicolet NEXUS-470 apparatus fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, U.S.A), The morphologies were investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100). We measure fluorescence performance with Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA).

QDs were susceptible to interference from surrounding environment, so we applied silica to protect it. Since silicon had a preeminent performance in optical transparency, chemical inertness and better distribution performance in aqueous phase. In addition, it was easy to modify various functional groups on SiO2 surface. It had been widely used in the field of optical materials modification. [27,28]

First of all, APBA served as the functional monomer to combine with DA, the red CdTe QDs were used as a response signal of DR-MIPs. The specific synthesis process was shown in Fig. 1 . A certain amount of CdTe QDs (for about 800 μL) was dispersed in distilled water (10 mL). Then APBA (20 mg) was added by ultrasonic dissolution and be stirred at room temperature for 12 h. After reaction, the product (CdTe QDs-APBA) was collected by centrifugation, then dissolved again with distilled water (10 mL). After that a certain amount of CQDs (approximately 50 μL) were added by stirring evenly until the color of the solution become deep pink. Next, the target DA (10 mg) was added in solution under stirring for 2 h, then the crosslink TEOS (100 μL) was added in the mixture under stirring for 20 min again. After that, NH3·H2O (100 μL) was added by dehydration condensation for 12 h to complete the silicon imprinting process. At last, DR-MIPs was collected by centrifugation, then washed repeatedly with ethyl alcohol and distilled water. After that, the sensor was eluted with 100 mL of acetic acid/methanol (1:9, v/v) to dislodge DA-template by Soxhlet extractor for 3 d, then dried at 40 °C under vacuum overnight.

The preparation process of double ratio molecularly non-imprinted polymers (DR-NIPs) was similar to DR-MIPs except not include DA. In addition, the preparation process of CdTe QDs-APBA@SiO2 was similar to DR-MIPs except not include CQDs.

In order to ensure the accuracy of the experiment, some preparations should be made before the fluorescence experiment. All fluorescence experiments were carried out at room temperature and fluorescence intensity was measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (the excitation wavelength was 330 nm). A certain amount of DR-MIPs (2 g/L) was dispersed in colorimetric tube as a reserve liquid. 10 mM of DA, catechol and gallic acid solution were allocated respectively. The reserve liquid (200 μL) was dissolved in 5 mL distilled water, then added to the above the DA, catechol and gallic acid solution respectively. After that, begin to take fluorescent detection. In order to carry out the visual detection of DA, all the test tubes were placed under an ultraviolet lamp with 365 nm wavelength, and take photos in darkness.

In this study, the blue emission CQDs was used as the interior label material of the ratiometric fluorescence sensors, and red emission CdTe QDs-APBA was used as a response signal. To show the selectivity of the probe, molecular imprinting technique was choosing to introduce into the probe. A simplest method called sol-gel method was adopted, briefly, CdTe QDs and CQDs as two fluorescent groups, APBA for the functional monomer and TEOS for the crosslink to synthesize DR-MIPs, finally, realized the visual inspection of DA. Fig. 2A showed the TEM morphologies of DR-MIPs. Both CQDs and CdTe QDs-APBA successfully embedded in the SiO2 nanospheres. Moreover, the synthesized SiO2 nanospheres have good dispersion and uniform size with a diameter of about 40 nm.

The FT-IR spectra of DR-MIPs were displayed in Fig. 2B. It can be seen from red curve, the peaks directed at bending and symmetric stretching vibration of 794 cm−1 was the characteristic signals of SiO, and peak at 1064 cm−1 was directed at asymmetric stretching of SiOSi. The characteristic peak at 2933 cm−1 was CH stretching vibration. The peak at 1564 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN bond. These could also explain the presence of amino. The results of the FT-IR spectra indicated that the SiO2 polymers successfully synthesized on the surface of the CQDs and CdTe QDs.

In this work, fluorescence detection of ratiometric fluorescent probes was achieved by simultaneously detecting the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and CdTe QDs. As could be seen from Fig. 3 , red-emission CdTe QDs (a) and blue-emission CQDs (c) exhibited a strong fluorescence emission at 448 nm and 651 nm respectively. The ratiometric fluorescent probes was prepared by introducing two kinds of quantum dots embedded together. At the same excitation wavelength, the DR-MIPs (b) had a distinct emission peak at 448 nm and 651 nm. The inset showed the photographs of (a) red-emission CdTe QDs, (b) DR-MIPs and (c) CQDs under daylight and a 365 nm UV lamp, and they showed red, dark pink and blue fluorescence respectively.

For another, the stability of DR-MIPs was measured by the duplicate detection of fluorescence intensity for 12 times within 120 min from Fig. S1. This fluorescence result showed that the DR-MIPs was defended by the SiO2 shell which generated good stability of the ratiometric fluorescent sensors, and it can be used to detect the content of DA steadily.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of pH and detection time
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the influence of pH value on the fluorescence intensity and detection system of DR-MIPs and DR-NIPs. As could be seen from Fig. 4(a), under acidic conditions, the fluorescence intensity of DR-MIPs and DR-NIPs is obviously quenched. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity decreased continuously in the range of pH to 10.0–13. When pH value was 9.0, the fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum. Therefore, chose 9.0 as the pH value for subsequent experiments.

In order to investigate the influence of the detection time on the fluorescence intensity, the DA (5 μM) was added into DR-MIPs, the fluorescence intensity (I448/I651)0/(I448/I651) was recorded at different interval time. Showed as Fig. 4, the relative fluorescence intensity of the test solution decreased rapidly within 2 min at first, when detection time was up to 2 min, the fluorescence intensity almost has no change. Therefore, 2 min was chosen as the optimum determination time for the following fluorescent tests.

The fluorescence capability and visual detection were important evidence for evaluating the selective recognition and detection sensitivity of DR-MIPs. Before adjunction of DA, DR-MIPs and DR-NIPs all had two distinct emission peaks at 448 nm and 651 nm respectively. After introduction of DA, with the increase of DA concentration, the fluorescence intensity of red-emission CdTe QDs at 651 nm decreased gradually, but the fluorescence intensity of blue-emission CQDs at 448 nm was relatively stable. As shown in Fig. 5(a), with the increasing of the quencher concentration from 0 to 5 μM, the fluorescence intensity of DR-MIPs decreased continuously. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of DR-MIPs and the concentration of DA was obtained by calculating the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 448 nm to 651 nm. Linear relation was Log(I448/I651)/(I448/I651)0 = 0.16729CDA − 0.05496 and correlation coefficient was 0.9861 (Fig. S2). Therefore, the DR-MIPs could detect DA quantitatively in the 0–5 μM concentration range, and LOD was 0.2853 μM. In addition, with the increasing of DA, the color of DR-MIPs turned from dark pink to blue under 365 nm UV lamp could also be observed very clearly in Fig. 5(a) inset. From the result, DA could be detected by visual color changes.

In order to show the superiority of DR-MIPs more intuitively, contrast experiments (DR-NIPs and CdTe QDs-APBA@SiO2) were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5b, DR-NIPs response to DA was relatively slow. Its fluorescence intensity decreased with the concentration of DA, but the decrease was not obvious. Therefore, as shown in Fig. 5(b) illustration the changes in color was not obvious. Thus, it was fully explained that APBA as the DA acceptor, and due to the usage of molecular imprinting techniques, the polymer leaved specific recognition sites of DA on the surface after elution of the template molecule. Therefore, DA could produce the maximum fluorescence quenching effect on the APBA modified CdTe QDs. From Fig. 5(c), DA could also quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs-APBA@SiO2, and the quenching extent was almost the same as that of DR-MIPs. The linear relation of CdTe QDs-APBA@SiO2 was Log(F0/F) = 0.1398CDA − 0.06904 and correlation coefficient was 0.98741 (Fig. S2). Obviously, the visual observation of CdTe QDs-APBA@SiO2 was greatly inferior to DR-MIPs, which attributed to that the same color was difficult to distinguish visually. For the reason of that ratio fluorescence probes consisting of two quantum dots (CQDs and CdTe QDs), the probes could be used for visual detection.

In order to apply this method to the actual detection, the ion interferences in the surrounding environment would also affect the detection results. Therefore, it was necessary to investigate the ion interferences on the detection results. Here, we chose eight common ions (Al3+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, K+, Mn2+ and Ba2+) in human serum as interfering ions. As the result in Fig. 6 , eight ions (3.5 μM) and DA were added to DR-MIPs and DR-NIPs respectively. From Fig. 6, these ions effects on DR-MIPs could be negligible. Fluorescence quenching of DR-MIPs by DA was consistent with the previous ones. Only DA produced the maximum fluorescence quenching of the DR-MIPs and changed the visual color of the solution. It proved that DR-MIPs could be used for the detection of real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cis-Diol substance detection of DR-MIPs detection system
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate whether DR-MIPs had any recognition effect on other adjacent hydroxyl groups, we chose gallate and catechol as the investigated substances. APBA acted as the DA acceptor, as a result there were covalent bonds formed between the two. The modified CdTe QDs-APBA was quenched by DA due to the specific covalent-binding reaction of the boronic acid groups with the two pairs cis-diols of DA. As was known, the existence of two pairs cis-diols in catechol and gallate were the key structural characteristics. As could be seen from Fig. 7 , the CdTe QDs in DR-MIPs was modified by APBA, catechol and gallate could also be identified, but their degree of quenching was significantly lower than the degree of DA on DR-MIPs. That is to say, the same concentration of other cis-diol substances could also cause changes on the fluorescence intensity of DR-MIPs, but the effect was not obvious, which owing to the specific identification sites on DR-MIPs. The further detection experiments of DR-MIPs in different kinds of solution were conducted, the results could be seen in Fig. S3. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence sensors could be applied to different fields.

To verify the usefulness of DR-MIPs, the DR-MIPs was used to detect the content of DA in human serum in this study. The amount of DA in human serum could be very little, the standard recovery method was adopted. First, collected human serum, the serum glucose was removed with the Fehling's reagent to exclude the effect of glucose on the test results, then diluted the rest human serum 100 times. Sample was divided into 6 groups (3 groups added standard, 3 groups without standard). In order to reflect the accuracy and preciseness of the experiment, the same process was repeated three times, and taking the average value. Table 1 listed the corresponding results. From the results, it illustrated that the DR-MIPs could detect DA in actual samples. This result indicated intelligibly that the DR-MIPs as rapid and efficient sensor could be used in determination human serum samples. Nowadays, there were serval methods had been reported to detect DA, some typical methods of DA detection were listed on Table 2 to have a comparation with colorimetric method used in this work, account on the synthesis and detection methods, the colorimetric method of this work had good visualization effects and relatively low detection limits, even the synthetic method of this work maybe comparatively facile and convent. Therefore, the colorimetric method could be a promising method for detecting DA content in living organisms.

In summary, double ratio molecularly imprinted polymers (DR-MIPs) as rapid and efficient detector were synthesized successfully and be used in visual detection. The synthesized sensor was proved not only smart and simple for visual optical determination, but also extended the application range of molecularly imprinted materials. The above fluorescence experiments revealed that the materials had good visual determination ability and sensitivity. The actual human serum analysis experiments illustrated that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor has good prospect of practical application. As a result, the potential prospects of this smart ratiometric fluorescence sensors demonstrated clearly in visual determination analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21507045, 21407057, U1407123, 21607013 and 21576111), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (Grant No. BK20160569, BK20140535, BK20140580), National Postdoctoral Science Foundation (1401108C, 2014M561595 and 2015M581744) and Postgraduate Innovation Programs Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX17_1793).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Detailed experimental characterization for polymers such as the fluorescent equation, measurements, and additional figures (result of stabilities, regenerative- experiments). Supplementary data to this article can be found online at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.04.035.
